content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 16, 1977


John R. Heisner
Deputy District Attorney
County of San Diego
County Courthouse
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-013

Dear Mr. Heisner:

Thank you for your letter of April 12, 1977, requesting an opinion with respect to whether Supervisor Roger Hedgecock will incur any reporting obligations pursuant to the Political Reform Act by virtue of establishing an office fund committee. Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful.

The facts, as I understand them, are as follows: after his election last December, Supervisor Hedgecock and his controlled committee completed their campaign activities and filed postelection campaign statements. Since that time, the controlled committee has not received or expended funds. However, the supervisor has established a separate entity to receive and expend funds in connection with his official duties as a county supervisor. This entity will expend funds for such items as newsletters, office supplies and travel expenses to and from conferences and meetings. None of the funds will be used for the purpose of expressly urging voters to support the supervisor in an election.

The Political Reform Act requires that donations received by or on behalf of an officeholder be reported as either gifts or political contributions (see Government Code Sections 84200, et seq.; 87200, et seq.).1/ If gifts, they must be reported on the officeholder's personal Statement of Economic Interest if worth $25 or more. Moreover, the officeholder may be required to disqualify himself from making or

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

29

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

John R. Heisner
Page two
June 16, 1977

participating in governmental decisions which could foresee-
ably and materially affect the donor of the gift.

The Act provides specifically however, that politi-
cal contributions are not to be considered gift income
(Section 82030(b)(1)). Contributions must be disclosed on
campaign statements in the manner set forth in Chapter 4 of
the Act. Unlike personal financial interest, political
contributions do not trigger any disqualification provisions
under the Act. Thus, the determinative question is whether
the office fund is political or personal in nature within
the meaning of the Political Reform Act.

Section 82015 of the Act defines contribution in
pertinent part as follows:

     "Contribution" means a payment, a forgive-
     ness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a
     third party, or an enforceable promise to
     make a payment except to the extent that
     full and adequate consideration is received
     unless it is clear from the surrounding cir-
     cumstances that it is not made for political
     purposes.

The term "political purposes" as used in that section refers
to more than the traditional campaign notion of attempting to
influence the voters for or against a candidate in connection
with an election. It also refers to actions made for the pur-
pose of assisting the officeholder to perform his duties as
a politician. This interpretation recognizes that elected
officeholders seek to communicate with constituents and other
governmental officials as a regular part of their "political"
activities. The Commission ratified this concept of political
purpose in opinions issued to Mayor Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC
Opinions 210 (No. 75-145, December 4, 1975), and Assemblyman
Willie Brown, 1 FPPC Opinions 67 (No. 75-055, July 2, 1975),
in which the Commission stated that payments received by the
officeholders for their committees in connection with their
official duties were political in nature. Thus, such payments
were not reportable as gifts on the officeholder's Statement
of Economic Interest but rather must be reported only on the
officer's campaign statements. The fact that such payments
were not used for the express purpose of influencing voters
did not cause the funds to lose their political character and
became personal in nature. Moreover, it would make little
sense as a matter of policy to construe such donations as gifts
rather than contributions in light of the long established prac-
tice of officeholders creating committees to raise funds for

John R. Heisner
Page three
June 16, 1977


use in connection with their official duties.

     Accordingly, we think the office fund is receiving
and expending funds in connection with Supervisor Hedgecock's
political activities even though such funds are not used for
the express purpose of influencing the voters. Therefore,
the office fund is a committee pursuant to Section 82013(a)
at the time it receives $500 or more in contributions. The
provisions of Chapter 4 of the Act should be consulted with
respect to the applicable filing requirements for this com-
mittee.

     If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321. If you have any questions, please contact Ted Prim
of the Commission's Legal Division. Again, we apologize for
the long delay in responding to your question.

                                        Sincerely,
                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:TP:jo

OFFICE OF

THE DISTRICT ATTORNEY
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO
EDWIN L. MILLER, JR.
DISTRICT ATTORNEY

April 12, 1977

Request No. _________
Date Received 2/18/77
Response Due _______
COUNTY COURTHOUSE
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101

XXXXX3X329
(714) 236-2423

Richard Miller, Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Opinion Request

Dear Dick:

Roger Hedgecock is a recently elected San Diego County
Supervisor. He has formed the Roger Hedgecock Committee,
a separate entity from his previous Campaign Committee,
for the declared purposes of accepting contributions and
making expenditures to finance a newsletter, pay for
travel expenses to political and governmental functions
outside the County, and pay for other miscellaneous
expenses incurred as a part of Mr. Hedgecock's function
as supervisor. Mr. Hedgecock is not presently a declared
candidate for office and has stated that the Roger Hedgecock Committee will not function as a campaign committee
nor will it contribute to or commingle funds with
Mr. Hedgecock's Campaign Committee.

In line with my duties as Enforcement Authority and Civil
Prosecutor under the Political Reform Act, I am requesting
an opinion covering the following questions:

1. Is this entity a "committee," as defined
in California Government Code Section 82013,
which must make disclosure under Chapter 4 of
the Act?

2. Assuming the answer to question 1 is in
the negative, would it be changed if the entity
used its funds to hire a professional public
relations consultant whose duties would include:

Richard Miller
Page 2
April 12, 1977

a. Gathering information and writing
the newsletter referred to above;

b. Contacting local and state media and
arranging publicity;

c. Advising Mr. Hedgecock on public
relations matters;

d. Writing news releases;

e. See attachment.

3. Notwithstanding the answer to the above,
must Mr. Hedgecock report the Committee's
assets as "income" under Chapter 7?

I have already issued an opinion to Mr. Hedgecock that his
organization would not be bound by our local ordinances as
originally constituted. However, the addition of a public
relations aide indicates to me that this body will lend
itself to use as a "de facto" committee, thus possibly
becoming subject to the requirements of the Political
Reform Act. I would appreciate your thoughts on the
matter.

Very truly yours,

EDWIN L. MILLER, JR.
District Attorney

By: [signature]
JOHN R. HEISNER
Deputy District Attorney

JRH:bl
Attach.

sections.question: 1. Is this entity a "committee," as defined in California Government Code Section 82013, which must make disclosure under Chapter A of the Act? 2. Assuming the answer to question 1 is in the negative, would it be changed if the entity used its funds to hire a professional public relations consultant whose duties would include: a. Gathering information and writing the newsletter referred to above, b. Contacting local and state media and arranging publicity; c. Advising Mr. Hedgecock on public relations matters; d. Writing news releases; e. See attachment. 3. Notwithstanding the answer to the above, must Mr. Hedgecock report the Committee's assets as "income" under Chapter 72
sections.conclusion: Accordingly, we think the office fund is receiving and expending funds in connection with supervisor Hedgecock's political activities even though such funds are not used for the express purpose of influencing the voters. Therefore, the office fund is a committee pursuant to section 5201.3(5) at the time it receives $500 or more in contributions. The provisions of chapter 4 of the Act should be consulted with respect to the applicable filing requirements for this committee.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Supervisor Hedgecock's office fund committee constitutes a reportable 'committee' under the Political Reform Act requiring disclosure, and whether funds used to hire a public relations consultant would change that determination.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the office fund constitutes a political committee subject to reporting requirements under the Act when it receives $500 or more in contributions, because funds used for official duties are political in nature even if not used to expressly influence voters.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (April 12, 1977) from San Diego County District Attorney and the FPPC response letter (June 16, 1977). The FPPC notes it is issuing informal comments rather than a formal opinion, as the request raises no substantial questions requiring formal interpretation. Some OCR artifacts present but content is clearly readable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is this entity a "committee," as defined in California Government Code Section 82013, which must make disclosure under Chapter A of the Act? 2. Assuming the answer to question 1 is in the negative, would it be changed if the entity used its funds to hire a professional public relations consultant whose duties would include: a. Gathering information and writing the newsletter referred to above, b. Contacting local and state media and arranging publicity; c. Advising Mr. Hedgecock on public relations matters; d. Writing news releases; e. See attachment. 3. Notwithstanding the answer to the above, must Mr. Hedgecock report the Committee's assets as "income" under Chapter 72

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, we think the office fund is receiving and expending funds in connection with supervisor Hedgecock's political activities even though such funds are not used for the express purpose of influencing the voters. Therefore, the office fund is a committee pursuant to section 5201.3(5) at the time it receives $500 or more in contributions. The provisions of chapter 4 of the Act should be consulted with respect to the applicable filing requirements for this committee.

embedding.summary: The FPPC addressed whether Supervisor Hedgecock's office fund committee must comply with Political Reform Act disclosure requirements. The Commission determined that the office fund is a political committee subject to reporting obligations when it receives $500 or more in contributions, as funds used in connection with official duties are political in nature.
